claims.claim_text: Ansible is an agentless automation platform used for configuration management, application deployment, cloud provisioning, and multi-node orchestration over SSH.
claims.span_hint: section: Ansible vs. Terraform / Ansible Architecture
claims.rationale: The upstream Ansible repository README directly describes these core platform capabilities and its agentless SSH model.

claims.claim_text: Windows nodes are commonly managed with WinRM or PSRP rather than the default SSH transport used on POSIX hosts.
claims.span_hint: section: Ansible Architecture > Agentless Design diagram
claims.rationale: This is a current Ansible transport-behavior claim best backed by Ansible's Windows documentation, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Managed nodes do not need Ansible installed, but POSIX nodes typically need Python available to execute Ansible-generated code.
claims.span_hint: section: Ansible Architecture > Agentless Design requirements
claims.rationale: This is an implementation detail from Ansible's installation and managed-node requirements docs, which are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Ansible modules and playbooks are intended to be idempotent so repeated runs converge on the same desired state.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story > What Ansible Provided / Key Takeaways
claims.rationale: The idempotency behavior is documented in Ansible's playbook guide, but that host is not on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Ansible check mode runs a dry run that reports intended changes without modifying remote systems.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story > What Ansible Provided / Common Mistakes / Hands-On Exercise
claims.rationale: Check mode is a concrete Ansible feature and should be cited from the official feature documentation.

claims.claim_text: Handlers run only when notified and repeated notifications are coalesced rather than causing repeated restarts.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story > What Ansible Provided / Ansible Roles / Key Takeaways
claims.rationale: This is a specific Ansible execution semantic documented in the official handlers guide.

claims.claim_text: The serial keyword controls batch size for rolling updates, allowing staged deployments across a host group.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story > What Ansible Provided / Advanced Playbook Patterns / Key Takeaways
claims.rationale: This is a precise Ansible playbook feature documented in the rolling-updates guidance.

claims.claim_text: Ansible Vault encrypts variables and files so secrets are not stored as plaintext in playbooks and roles.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do / Common Mistakes / Key Takeaways
claims.rationale: Secret-handling behavior is a concrete product capability and should cite the official Vault documentation.

claims.claim_text: The amazon.aws.aws_ec2 inventory plugin can build EC2-backed dynamic inventory and derive groups from instance tags and other metadata.
claims.span_hint: section: Inventory Management > AWS EC2 Plugin (Recommended)
claims.rationale: This is a specific inventory-plugin capability documented in the official collection docs, which are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The kubernetes.core collection includes modules such as k8s, k8s_info, helm, and helm_repository for managing Kubernetes resources.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do / Ansible for Kubernetes
claims.rationale: The collection's upstream README enumerates these modules and their Kubernetes-management purpose.

claims.claim_text: The Terraform Kubernetes provider supports full lifecycle management of Kubernetes resources.
claims.span_hint: section: Ansible vs. Terraform table row 'Manage Kubernetes resources'
claims.rationale: The provider's upstream README states that it enables full lifecycle management of Kubernetes resources.

claims.claim_text: Molecule is an Ansible testing framework for collections, playbooks, and roles.
claims.span_hint: section: Testing Ansible with Molecule
claims.rationale: The Molecule upstream README directly describes it as an Ansible testing framework for these artifact types.

claims.claim_text: A configuration management console showed exactly 2,847 servers in an unknown state.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: *The configuration management console showed 2,847 servers in "unknown" state.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *The configuration management console showed a large portion of the fleet in an unknown state.*
claims.rationale: This is a precise but unattributed anecdotal number and should be rewritten to preserve the lesson without fabricated specificity.

claims.claim_text: An e-commerce company facing Black Friday traffic 400% above normal had manually configured servers over three years and no one knew the exact machine state.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: When the e-commerce company's Black Friday traffic projections came in—400% above normal—their infrastructure team realized the nightmare scenario: their servers were configured manually over three years. No one knew the exact state of any machine. Some had been patched, others hadn't. Some ran different application versions. Some had configuration drift that would cause failures under load.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a team discovers heavy configuration drift across manually managed servers ahead of a high-traffic event, the lack of a reliable source of truth quickly becomes a major operational risk.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced operational anecdote with named context and exact percentages that should not remain as factual history.

claims.claim_text: The team had six weeks to standardize nearly 3,000 servers, equivalent to 71 servers per day with zero errors allowed.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: They had six weeks to achieve configuration parity across nearly 3,000 servers. Manual intervention was impossible—that's 71 servers per day, with zero errors allowed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: They had a short window to standardize a very large fleet, which made manual intervention unrealistic.
claims.rationale: The exact fleet size and throughput math are unattributed anecdotal specifics that should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Ansible let the team audit every server, document drift, finish in 23 days, avoid Black Friday failures, and support $127 million in revenue over 24 hours.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: **Ansible** became their salvation. In 23 days, they wrote playbooks that audited every server's state, documented all drift, and enforced consistent configuration. On Black Friday, zero servers failed due to configuration issues. Revenue: $127 million in 24 hours.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Ansible** gave the team a way to audit drift, document server state, and enforce consistent configuration before a critical traffic event.
claims.rationale: This is a fabricated-style success story with exact duration, failure count, and revenue figures that cannot be left as fact without a source.

claims.claim_text: A critical OpenSSL vulnerability with a placeholder CVE identifier forced a 72-hour patching deadline across 1,200 servers before public exploitation.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Patch That Broke Production
claims.anchor_text: A critical OpenSSL vulnerability (CVE-2024-XXXX) was announced. Marcus had 72 hours to patch all 1,200 servers before the exploit went public.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A critical OpenSSL vulnerability was announced, and the team needed to patch a large server fleet quickly before public exploitation spread.
claims.rationale: The placeholder CVE and exact deadline/fleet size make this an unsalvageable pseudo-incident as written.

claims.claim_text: Marcus was a senior SRE with 8 years of experience.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Patch That Broke Production > Characters
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Incident lead: senior SRE coordinating the response
claims.rationale: This is fictional-seeming character detail with precise experience that has no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The team consisted of 6 engineers managing 1,200 servers.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Patch That Broke Production > Characters
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Team: small engineering group managing a large server fleet
claims.rationale: These precise team and fleet numbers are part of an unattributed anecdote and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: NASA has publicly presented Ansible use in its High-End Computing environment.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 1, first sentence
claims.rationale: NASA's NTRS record is the relevant primary source, but nasa.gov/NTRS is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Agentless architecture was crucial for NASA's security requirements because it added no extra attack surface on compute nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 1, second sentence
claims.anchor_text: The agentless architecture was crucial for their security requirements—no additional attack surface on compute nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Agentless automation can matter in locked-down environments because it avoids installing extra software on managed nodes.
claims.rationale: The specific causal security rationale is unattributed and should be rewritten as a general principle rather than a claimed NASA case fact.

claims.claim_text: The name 'Ansible' comes from Ursula K. Le Guin's fiction, where an ansible is an instantaneous communication device.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 2
claims.rationale: The referenced history and naming origin are directly summarized on the cited general-reference page.

claims.claim_text: Ansible Galaxy has over 30,000 roles shared by the community.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Ansible Galaxy has over 30,000 roles** shared by the community. Before writing a role from scratch, check Galaxy—there's probably a well-tested role for your use case.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Ansible Galaxy hosts a large catalog of community roles and collections.** Before writing a role from scratch, check Galaxy—there may already be a well-tested option for your use case.
claims.rationale: The exact count is time-sensitive and not backed by an allowlisted live source here, so the statistic should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Red Hat acquired Ansible in 2015 for $150 million.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 4, first sentence
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Red Hat acquired Ansible in 2015** for $150 million.
claims.rationale: Red Hat's own materials support the acquisition year but not this precise price figure, so the exact amount should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Red Hat announced Red Hat Ansible Automation Platform in 2019 as an enterprise automation offering.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know item 4, second sentence
claims.rationale: The cited Red Hat press release directly documents the AAP announcement and its positioning as an enterprise automation platform.

further_reading.title: Ansible upstream repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for Ansible's core architecture, capabilities, and release history.

further_reading.title: kubernetes.core collection
further_reading.why_relevant: Upstream source for the Ansible Kubernetes collection used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Terraform upstream repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for Terraform's declarative infrastructure model and lifecycle-oriented workflow.

notes: Quiz items, exercise steps, code blocks, and most table rows were excluded per schema unless nearby prose made a concrete capability claim. Repeated takeaways were collapsed into the same capability claims rather than duplicated for every restatement.
